sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A Doppler ultrasound probe emitted 5.00 MHz sound waves into an artery at an angle of 60° relative to blood flow. The reflected signal was shifted by 4.00 kHz. For backscattered ultrasound, the approximate Doppler shift is Δf = 2f0v cosθ/c, where c = 1540 m/s in soft tissue. The clinician used the shift to estimate blood speed and repeated the measurement at several sites along the vessel.
questions.stem: What blood speed corresponds to a 4.00 kHz Doppler shift?

explanations.A: Correct. Rearrange Δf = 2f0v cosθ/c. v = Δf c/(2f0 cosθ) = (4000)(1540)/(2 × 5.00×10^6 × 0.5) ≈ 1.23 m/s.
explanations.B: 0.62 m/s would result from an extra factor of two in the denominator.
explanations.C: 2.46 m/s ignores the cosine factor or handles the backscatter factor incorrectly.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Predicted Doppler frequency shift for a 5.00 MHz probe at 60° in soft tissue.

questions.stem: Why does the equation include cosθ?

choices.D: Only the velocity component along the ultrasound beam contributes to the observed shift.
choices.A: The sound speed changes into the cosine of the angle.
choices.B: The blood stops moving perpendicular to the probe.

explanations.D: Correct. Doppler shift depends on motion toward or away from the transducer. The cosθ term projects the blood velocity onto the direction of the ultrasound beam.
explanations.A: The speed of sound in tissue is not replaced by cosθ.
explanations.B: Blood can still have perpendicular motion; it simply does not contribute to the beam-directed shift.
explanations.C: Unit conversion is unrelated to the trigonometric projection.

questions.stem: What happens to Δf if the probe angle increases toward 90° while speed is fixed?

explanations.B: Correct. As θ approaches 90°, cosθ approaches zero. The velocity component along the beam becomes very small, so the Doppler shift decreases toward zero.
explanations.A: The cosine term decreases rather than increases.
explanations.C: The equation shows explicit dependence on θ.
explanations.D: Velocity still matters, but the measured component becomes small near 90°.

questions.stem: Why is ultrasound useful for measuring flow speed?

choices.C: Reflected sound changes frequency when scattered by moving blood cells.
choices.A: Blood cells emit visible light at MHz frequencies.
choices.B: Stationary tissue always produces the largest shift.
choices.D: Sound waves cannot reflect from biological structures.

explanations.C: Correct. Moving scatterers produce a Doppler frequency shift in reflected ultrasound. Measuring that shift allows estimation of velocity along the beam direction.
explanations.A: MHz describes sound frequency here, not visible light emission.
explanations.B: Stationary tissue produces little or no Doppler shift.
explanations.D: Ultrasound imaging relies on reflection and scattering in tissues.

questions.stem: Which change would double the Doppler shift if all else stayed constant?

choices.C: Halving f0 while velocity is fixed

explanations.D: Correct. The Doppler shift is directly proportional to velocity. If v doubles and f0, θ, and c are unchanged, Δf doubles.
explanations.A: Halving velocity would halve the shift.
explanations.B: At 90°, cosθ is zero, so the shift would collapse rather than double.
explanations.C: Halving f0 would halve the shift.

questions.passage: A researcher measured sound intensity near a laboratory centrifuge. At 1.0 m from the device, the sound level was 70 dB. After acoustic panels were installed, the level at the same location fell to 60 dB. The decibel scale is β = 10 log(I/I0). A 10 dB decrease corresponds to a tenfold decrease in intensity. Students also noted that perceived loudness does not scale linearly with physical intensity.
questions.stem: A decrease from 70 dB to 60 dB corresponds to what change in intensity?

explanations.A: Correct. On the decibel scale, a 10 dB decrease means intensity is reduced by a factor of 10. The panels therefore lowered physical intensity to one-tenth of the original value at the measurement point.
explanations.B: A twofold change is not what 10 dB represents.
explanations.C: The level changed by 10 dB, so intensity did not stay constant.
explanations.D: The decibel value decreased, so intensity decreased rather than increased.

questions.stem: If intensity decreases by a factor of 100, how many dB lower is the sound level?

explanations.B: Correct. β changes by 10 log(I2/I1). If I2/I1 = 1/100, the change is 10 log(0.01) = -20 dB. Thus the level is 20 dB lower.
explanations.A: 10 dB corresponds to a factor of 10, not 100.
explanations.C: 2 dB is much too small for a hundredfold intensity change.
explanations.D: 100 dB confuses intensity ratio with decibel difference.

questions.stem: Why is the decibel scale logarithmic?

choices.C: Sound intensities span many orders of magnitude.
choices.A: Sound intensity cannot be measured numerically.
choices.B: Frequency and intensity are the same variable.
choices.D: Logarithms make all sounds equally loud.

explanations.C: Correct. A logarithmic scale compresses very large intensity ranges into manageable numbers. This is useful because audible sound intensities vary across many powers of ten.
explanations.A: Intensity is measurable; the issue is the range of values.
explanations.B: Frequency and intensity describe different wave properties.
explanations.D: The logarithmic scale does not make sounds equally loud; it represents ratios compactly.

questions.stem: Which physical quantity is directly reduced by the acoustic panels?

choices.A: Sound intensity at the measurement point
choices.C: Wavelength of all sounds to zero

explanations.A: Correct. The passage reports a lower decibel level at the same location after panels were installed. That corresponds to reduced sound intensity reaching the meter.
explanations.B: The speed of sound in air is mostly determined by the medium and temperature, not acoustic panels.
explanations.C: Panels absorb or scatter sound; they do not reduce all wavelengths to zero.
explanations.D: The centrifuge's mass is not what was measured or changed.

questions.stem: Why might perceived loudness not fall by the same numerical factor as intensity?

choices.D: Sensory perception is not linearly proportional to physical intensity.
choices.A: The intensity did not actually change.
choices.B: The ear measures only wavelength and not amplitude.
choices.C: Decibel equations apply only to light.

explanations.D: Correct. The passage notes that perceived loudness does not scale linearly with physical intensity. Human sensory systems often encode stimuli in nonlinear ways, so a tenfold intensity decrease does not necessarily feel exactly ten times quieter.
explanations.A: The decibel drop indicates a real intensity change.
explanations.B: The auditory system is sensitive to amplitude-related pressure variations as well as frequency.
explanations.C: Decibel equations are widely used for sound intensity.
